question: 3. Find all complex-valued functions \( f \) of a complex variable such that \( f(z)+z f(1-z)=1+z \) for all \( z \).
solution:
Solution. We have
\[
f(z)+z f(1-z)=1+z
\]
for all \( z \). Replace \( z \) by \( 1-z \) in this equation to get
\[
f(1-z)+(1-z) f(z)=2-z
\]

Eliminating \( f(1-z) \) from (1) and (2), we get
\[
\left(1-z+z^{2}\right) f(z)=1-z+z^{2}
\]

Hence \( f(z)=1 \) for all \( z \) except possibly the two values \( e^{ \pm \pi i / 3} \) for which \( 1-z+z^{2}=0 \).

Let \( \alpha=e^{i \pi / 3}, \vec{\alpha}=e^{-i \pi / 3} \). Then \( \alpha+\bar{\alpha}=1, \alpha \bar{\alpha}=1 \). Let \( f(\alpha)=1+\beta \), \( f(\bar{\alpha})=1+\gamma \). Then equation (1) with \( z=\alpha \) becomes
\[
\beta+\alpha \gamma=0,
\]
so we choose \( \beta \) arbitrarily and set \( \gamma=-\bar{\alpha} \beta \). With these values (1) is readily checked for \( z=\alpha, \bar{\alpha} \). Hence a function \( f \) satisfies the given equation if and only if it has the form
\[
\begin{array}{l}
f(\alpha)=1+\beta \\
f(\bar{\alpha})=1-\bar{\alpha} \beta
\end{array}
\]
\[
f(z)=1 \text { for all other values of } z,
\]
where \( \beta \) is any complex number.

descriptive_long.question: 3. Find all complex-valued functions \( complexfunc \) of a complex variable such that \( complexfunc(complexvar)+complexvar complexfunc(1-complexvar)=1+complexvar \) for all \( complexvar \).
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. We have
\[
complexfunc(complexvar)+complexvar complexfunc(1-complexvar)=1+complexvar
\]
for all \( complexvar \). Replace \( complexvar \) by \( 1-complexvar \) in this equation to get
\[
complexfunc(1-complexvar)+(1-complexvar) complexfunc(complexvar)=2-complexvar
\]

Eliminating complexfunc(1-complexvar) from (1) and (2), we get
\[
\left(1-complexvar+complexvar^{2}\right) complexfunc(complexvar)=1-complexvar+complexvar^{2}
\]

Hence \( complexfunc(complexvar)=1 \) for all \( complexvar \) except possibly the two values \( e^{ \pm \pi i / 3} \) for which \( 1-complexvar+complexvar^{2}=0 \).

Let \( rootalpha=e^{i \pi / 3}, \vec{rootalpha}=e^{-i \pi / 3} \). Then \( rootalpha+\bar{rootalpha}=1, rootalpha \bar{rootalpha}=1 \). Let \( complexfunc(rootalpha)=1+coeffbeta \), \( complexfunc(\bar{rootalpha})=1+coeffgamma \). Then equation (1) with \( complexvar=rootalpha \) becomes
\[
coeffbeta+rootalpha coeffgamma=0,
\]
so we choose \( coeffbeta \) arbitrarily and set \( coeffgamma=-\bar{rootalpha} coeffbeta \). With these values (1) is readily checked for \( complexvar=rootalpha, \bar{rootalpha} \). Hence a function \( complexfunc \) satisfies the given equation if and only if it has the form
\[
\begin{array}{l}
complexfunc(rootalpha)=1+coeffbeta \\
complexfunc(\bar{rootalpha})=1-\bar{rootalpha} coeffbeta
\end{array}
\]
\[
complexfunc(complexvar)=1 \text { for all other values of } complexvar,
\]
where \( coeffbeta \) is any complex number.

descriptive_long_confusing.question: 3. Find all complex-valued functions \( horizonline \) of a complex variable such that \( horizonline(pineapple)+pineapple horizonline(1-pineapple)=1+pineapple \) for all \( pineapple \).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. We have
\[
horizonline(pineapple)+pineapple horizonline(1-pineapple)=1+pineapple
\]
for all \( pineapple \). Replace \( pineapple \) by \( 1-pineapple \) in this equation to get
\[
horizonline(1-pineapple)+(1-pineapple) horizonline(pineapple)=2-pineapple
\]

Eliminating \( horizonline(1-pineapple) \) from (1) and (2), we get
\[
\left(1-pineapple+pineapple^{2}\right) horizonline(pineapple)=1-pineapple+pineapple^{2}
\]

Hence \( horizonline(pineapple)=1 \) for all \( pineapple \) except possibly the two values \( e^{ \pm \pi i / 3} \) for which \( 1-pineapple+pineapple^{2}=0 \).

Let \( starlight=e^{i \pi / 3}, \vec{starlight}=e^{-i \pi / 3} \). Then \( starlight+\bar{starlight}=1, starlight \bar{starlight}=1 \). Let \( horizonline(starlight)=1+moonshadow \), \( horizonline(\bar{starlight})=1+cloudburst \). Then equation (1) with \( pineapple=starlight \) becomes
\[
moonshadow+starlight cloudburst=0,
\]
so we choose \( moonshadow \) arbitrarily and set \( cloudburst=-\bar{starlight} moonshadow \). With these values (1) is readily checked for \( pineapple=starlight, \bar{starlight} \). Hence a function \( horizonline \) satisfies the given equation if and only if it has the form
\[
\begin{array}{l}
horizonline(starlight)=1+moonshadow \\
horizonline(\bar{starlight})=1-\bar{starlight} moonshadow
\end{array}
\]
\[
horizonline(pineapple)=1 \text { for all other values of } pineapple,
\]
where \( moonshadow \) is any complex number.

descriptive_long_misleading.question: 3. Find all complex-valued functions \( nonfunction \) of a complex variable such that \( nonfunction(realconstant)+realconstant\,nonfunction(1-realconstant)=1+realconstant \) for all \( realconstant \).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. We have
\[
nonfunction(realconstant)+realconstant\,nonfunction(1-realconstant)=1+realconstant
\]
for all \( realconstant \). Replace \( realconstant \) by \( 1-realconstant \) in this equation to get
\[
nonfunction(1-realconstant)+(1-realconstant)\,nonfunction(realconstant)=2-realconstant
\]

Eliminating \( nonfunction(1-realconstant) \) from (1) and (2), we get
\[
\left(1-realconstant+realconstant^{2}\right) nonfunction(realconstant)=1-realconstant+realconstant^{2}
\]

Hence \( nonfunction(realconstant)=1 \) for all \( realconstant \) except possibly the two values \( e^{ \pm \pi i / 3} \) for which \( 1-realconstant+realconstant^{2}=0 \).

Let \( lastletter=e^{i \pi / 3}, \vec{lastletter}=e^{-i \pi / 3} \). Then \( lastletter+\bar{lastletter}=1, lastletter\,\bar{lastletter}=1 \). Let \( nonfunction(lastletter)=1+startletter \), \( nonfunction(\bar{lastletter})=1+middleletter \). Then equation (1) with \( realconstant=lastletter \) becomes
\[
startletter+lastletter\,middleletter=0,
\]
so we choose \( startletter \) arbitrarily and set \( middleletter=-\bar{lastletter}\,startletter \). With these values (1) is readily checked for \( realconstant=lastletter, \bar{lastletter} \). Hence a function \( nonfunction \) satisfies the given equation if and only if it has the form
\[
\begin{array}{l}
nonfunction(lastletter)=1+startletter \\
nonfunction(\bar{lastletter})=1-\bar{lastletter}\,startletter
\end{array}
\]
\[
nonfunction(realconstant)=1 \text { for all other values of } realconstant,
\]
where \( startletter \) is any complex number.

garbled_string.question: Find all complex-valued functions \( hjgrksla \) of a complex variable such that \( hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp)+qzxwvtnp hjgrksla(1-qzxwvtnp)=1+qzxwvtnp \) for all \( qzxwvtnp \).
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. We have
\[
hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp)+qzxwvtnp hjgrksla(1-qzxwvtnp)=1+qzxwvtnp
\]
for all \( qzxwvtnp \). Replace \( qzxwvtnp \) by \( 1-qzxwvtnp \) in this equation to get
\[
hjgrksla(1-qzxwvtnp)+(1-qzxwvtnp) hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp)=2-qzxwvtnp
\]

Eliminating \( hjgrksla(1-qzxwvtnp) \) from (1) and (2), we get
\[
\left(1-qzxwvtnp+qzxwvtnp^{2}\right) hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp)=1-qzxwvtnp+qzxwvtnp^{2}
\]

Hence \( hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp)=1 \) for all \( qzxwvtnp \) except possibly the two values \( e^{ \pm \pi i / 3} \) for which \( 1-qzxwvtnp+qzxwvtnp^{2}=0 \).

Let \( plmsdfgh=e^{i \pi / 3}, \vec{plmsdfgh}=e^{-i \pi / 3} \). Then \( plmsdfgh+\bar{plmsdfgh}=1, plmsdfgh \bar{plmsdfgh}=1 \). Let \( hjgrksla(plmsdfgh)=1+rtyuiojk \), \( hjgrksla(\bar{plmsdfgh})=1+cvbnmqwe \). Then equation (1) with \( qzxwvtnp=plmsdfgh \) becomes
\[
rtyuiojk+plmsdfgh cvbnmqwe=0,
\]
so we choose \( rtyuiojk \) arbitrarily and set \( cvbnmqwe=-\bar{plmsdfgh} rtyuiojk \). With these values (1) is readily checked for \( qzxwvtnp=plmsdfgh, \bar{plmsdfgh} \). Hence a function \( hjgrksla \) satisfies the given equation if and only if it has the form
\[
\begin{array}{l}
hjgrksla(plmsdfgh)=1+rtyuiojk \\
hjgrksla(\bar{plmsdfgh})=1-\bar{plmsdfgh} rtyuiojk
\end{array}
\]
\[
hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp)=1 \text { for all other values of } qzxwvtnp,
\]
where \( rtyuiojk \) is any complex number.

kernel_variant.question:
Let  

  \Delta (z)=1-z^2+z^3 = \prod _{j=1}^{3}(z-\rho _j)  (\rho _1,\rho _2,\rho _3 pairwise distinct)  

and set S={\rho _1,\rho _2,\rho _3}.  

For an entire function f:\mathbb{C}\to \mathbb{C} consider the coupled system  

(I) f(z)+z\cdot f(1-z)=1+z  
(II) f(1-z)+(1-z)\cdot f(-z)=2-z  
(III) f(-z)-z\cdot f(z)=1-z          (*)

assumed to hold for every z\in \mathbb{C}.

1.  Prove that, for entire f, the system (*) is equivalent to the single algebraic identity  
    \Delta (z)\cdot [f(z)-1]=0   for all z\in \mathbb{C}.  

2.  Deduce that the unique entire (indeed meromorphic) solution of the full system (*) is the constant function f\equiv 1.

(Harder refinement - selective rigidity)

3.  Let g be an arbitrary entire function and suppose that g satisfies exactly one of the three relations (I)-(III).  
 (a)  Show that if g satisfies (I) then g\equiv 1.  
 (b)  Show that if g satisfies (III) then g\equiv 1.  
 (c)  Show that equation (II) admits non-constant entire solutions. Construct an explicit infinite-dimensional family of such solutions and prove that every function of this family indeed satisfies (II).  
 (d)  Conclude that the relations (I) and (III) are rigid (they force an entire solution to be the constant 1 and automatically imply the other two), whereas relation (II) is flexible: it has an infinite-dimensional space of entire solutions and, by itself, does not entail either (I) or (III).

4.  (Very hard)  Let  
 U := {z\in \mathbb{C} : {z,1-z}\cap S = \emptyset } = \mathbb{C}\bigl(S\cup (1-S)\bigr)  
and let h be a meromorphic function on U that satisfies (I) there.  
 (a) Prove that h is holomorphic at the two points  
   \zeta _+=e^{i\pi /3}, \zeta _-=e^{-i\pi /3}  (the zeros of D(z):=1-z+z^2).  
 (b) Show that h extends meromorphically through every \rho \in S and that the extension coincides with the constant 1; in particular h\equiv 1 on \mathbb{C}.

Determine all functions that satisfy the system (*) and provide complete, rigorous proofs of every assertion.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we use the matrices  

 M(z)=1  z  0   b(z)=1+z,   X(z)=f(z)  ,  
    0  1  1-z     2-z       f(1-z),  
    -z 0  1     1-z       f(-z)  ,

so that (*) is equivalent to M(z)X(z)=b(z).

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Equivalence (*) \Leftrightarrow  \Delta (z)[f(z)-1]=0    

Step 1. det M(z).  
A cofactor expansion gives  

  det M(z)=1-z^2+z^3=:\Delta (z).  (1)

Step 2. Cramer's rule for f(z).  
Replace the first column of M(z) by b(z) and call the new matrix M_1(z).  
Again by a cofactor expansion  

  det M_1(z)=1-z^2+z^3=\Delta (z).  

Hence  

  \Delta (z)\cdot f(z)=det M_1(z)=\Delta (z) \Rightarrow  \Delta (z)[f(z)-1]=0  for all z\in \mathbb{C}. (2)

Step 3. Converse.  
If an entire f satisfies (2) then f(z)=1 on \mathbb{C}\S, an open set possessing an
accumulation point; by the Identity Theorem f\equiv 1.  Substituting f\equiv 1
into (*) confirms the three relations, whence (*) and (2) are equivalent.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Uniqueness of the entire solution  

Part 1 already enforces f\equiv 1; consequently the full coupled system (I)-(III)
admits exactly one entire (indeed meromorphic) solution:

    f(z)=1  (\forall z\in \mathbb{C}).

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Selective rigidity

Put h(z):=g(z)-1.  The three relations become homogeneous equations for h.

3(a) Equation (I).  
(I) gives h(z)+z h(1-z)=0.  Replacing z by 1-z and eliminating h(1-z)
yields  

  (1-z+z^2) h(z)=0=:D(z) h(z).  

Because h is entire, D(z) h(z)\equiv 0 implies h vanishes identically: outside
its two isolated zeros \zeta _+,\zeta _- of D the factor in front of h is non-zero,
and the Identity Theorem forces h\equiv 0.  Hence g\equiv 1.

3(b) Equation (III).  
(III) becomes  

  h(-z)-z h(z)=0.  (3)

Replacing z by -z and substituting the first relation into the second one
gives  

  (1+z^2) h(z)=0.  

Again h is entire, so it must vanish identically; whence g\equiv 1.

3(c) Equation (II) - an infinite-dimensional solution space.  

The inhomogeneous equation  

  h(1-z)+(1-z) h(-z)=0   (4)

does not force h to vanish.  We construct an explicit family of
non-trivial entire solutions.  Let \Phi  be any entire function satisfying  

  \Phi (1-z)=\Phi (-z)  for every z\in \mathbb{C}.  (5)

(An abundance of such functions exists; for instance every exponential
\Phi _n(z)=e^{2\pi  i n z} (n\in \mathbb{Z}) has this property because
e^{2\pi  i n(1-z)}=e^{2\pi  i n}e^{-2\pi  i n z}=e^{-2\pi  i n z}=\Phi _n(-z).)

Define  

  h_\Phi (z):=\Gamma (z+1)\sin(\pi  z) \Phi (z),    (6)

and put  

  g_\Phi (z):=1+h_\Phi (z).        (7)

Entirety of h_\Phi .
\Gamma (z+1) has simple poles at the negative integers, while sin \pi z has the
same zeros; hence their product is an entire function.  Multiplying by
the entire \Phi  preserves entire-ness.

Verification of (4).  
We use the two standard formulas  

  \Gamma (2-z)=(1-z) \Gamma (1-z),    sin(\pi (1-z))=sin \pi z.  (8)

From (6) and (8) we obtain  

h_\Phi (1-z)=(1-z) \Gamma (1-z) sin(\pi  z) \Phi (1-z),  
h_\Phi (-z)=-\Gamma (1-z) sin(\pi  z) \Phi (-z).

Because \Phi  satisfies (5), \Phi (1-z)=\Phi (-z), whence  

h_\Phi (1-z)+(1-z) h_\Phi (-z)  
  = (1-z) \Gamma (1-z) sin(\pi  z) \Phi (-z)  
    -(1-z) \Gamma (1-z) sin(\pi  z) \Phi (-z)  = 0.

Thus every g_\Phi  defined in (7) satisfies equation (II).  Varying the
entire function \Phi  subject to (5) produces an infinite-dimensional family
of solutions; for \Phi \equiv 1 we recover the elementary example

  g(z)=1+\Gamma (z+1)\sin(\pi  z)

mentioned in the review.

Therefore equation (II) alone is flexible: it has infinitely many
entire solutions, none of which (except the constant one) satisfies
either (I) or (III).

3(d) Consequences.  
* Relations (I) and (III) are rigid: every entire solution equals 1;  
 this solution necessarily fulfils the other two relations, so the whole
 system (*) holds.  
* Relation (II) is flexible: it possesses an infinite-dimensional space
 of entire solutions and does not imply (I) or (III).

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Meromorphic solutions defined on U  

Put k(z)=h(z)-1.  Throughout we only use relation (I); hence again the
polynomial  

  D(z)=1-z+z^2  (9)

reappears.

Equation (I) reads on U  

  k(z)+z k(1-z)=0, (10)  

and replacing z by 1-z gives  

  k(1-z)+(1-z) k(z)=0. (11)

Eliminating k(1-z) from (10)-(11) gives  

  D(z) k(z)=0.   (12)

4(a) The zeros \zeta _+, \zeta _- of D.  
Outside the points \zeta \pm  the factor D(z) is non-zero, so (12) forces
k\equiv 0.  Near \zeta \pm  the same argument as in the original solution shows that h
is holomorphic and assumes the value 1 at each of \zeta \pm .

4(b) Extension across the roots of \Delta .  
Let \rho \in S.  Because \rho \notin U, h is a priori undefined at \rho , but U contains a
punctured neighbourhood of \rho .  On U we already know k\equiv 0, so
lim_{z\to \rho ,z\in U}k(z)=0.  If h had a pole at \rho  the same limit would be
infinite - contradiction.  Hence \rho  is a removable singularity of h with
value 1.  Doing this for every root of \Delta  yields an entire extension that
equals 1 on U and therefore everywhere.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Answer.  The coupled system (I)-(III) possesses exactly one entire---and in
fact one meromorphic---solution:

      f(z)=1  for every z\in \mathbb{C}. \blacksquare

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
•  Multiple equations  –   The original problem has a single
functional equation; here one must satisfy {\em three} coupled relations
simultaneously.

•  Three interacting arguments  –   
The unknown values \(f(z),\,f(1-z),\,f(-z)\) appear together, forcing a
\(3\times3\) linear system instead of the \(2\times2\) system in the
kernel variant.  This raises both algebraic complexity and bookkeeping.

•  Non-trivial determinant  –  
Solving demands computation of a cubic determinant
\(\Delta(z)=1-z^{2}+z^{3}\) whose vanishing introduces a {\em singular
locus} of three complex points.  One must treat the regular and
singular cases separately, recognise the rank drop, and manage the
resulting free parameters.

•  Rational–function core  –   
Outside the singular set the solution is a genuinely {\em rational}
function of degree three over degree three, rather than the constant
solution of the original problem.  Obtaining it requires systematic
elimination, not mere substitution.

•  Parametrisation on the singular set  –   
At each root of \(\Delta\) the system loses rank, so compatibility must be
checked and the local freedom carefully parametrised; this demands a
full analysis of linear dependence, something entirely absent in the
original exercise.

In summary, the problem keeps the original idea
“relate \(f\) at two (now three) symmetric points and isolate a
multiplicative polynomial”, but it escalates the dimension, the number
of constraints, the algebraic labour, and the subtlety of the singular
case, making it significantly harder than both the original and the
current kernel variant.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let  

  \Delta (z)=1-z^2+z^3 = \prod _{j=1}^{3}(z-\rho _j)  (\rho _1,\rho _2,\rho _3 pairwise distinct)  

and set S={\rho _1,\rho _2,\rho _3}.  

For an entire function f:\mathbb{C}\to \mathbb{C} consider the coupled system  

(I) f(z)+z\cdot f(1-z)=1+z  
(II) f(1-z)+(1-z)\cdot f(-z)=2-z  
(III) f(-z)-z\cdot f(z)=1-z          (*)

assumed to hold for every z\in \mathbb{C}.

1.  Prove that, for entire f, the system (*) is equivalent to the single algebraic identity  
    \Delta (z)\cdot [f(z)-1]=0   for all z\in \mathbb{C}.  

2.  Deduce that the unique entire (indeed meromorphic) solution of the full system (*) is the constant function f\equiv 1.

(Harder refinement - selective rigidity)

3.  Let g be an arbitrary entire function and suppose that g satisfies exactly one of the three relations (I)-(III).  
 (a)  Show that if g satisfies (I) then g\equiv 1.  
 (b)  Show that if g satisfies (III) then g\equiv 1.  
 (c)  Show that equation (II) admits non-constant entire solutions. Construct an explicit infinite-dimensional family of such solutions and prove that every function of this family indeed satisfies (II).  
 (d)  Conclude that the relations (I) and (III) are rigid (they force an entire solution to be the constant 1 and automatically imply the other two), whereas relation (II) is flexible: it has an infinite-dimensional space of entire solutions and, by itself, does not entail either (I) or (III).

4.  (Very hard)  Let  
 U := {z\in \mathbb{C} : {z,1-z}\cap S = \emptyset } = \mathbb{C}\bigl(S\cup (1-S)\bigr)  
and let h be a meromorphic function on U that satisfies (I) there.  
 (a) Prove that h is holomorphic at the two points  
   \zeta _+=e^{i\pi /3}, \zeta _-=e^{-i\pi /3}  (the zeros of D(z):=1-z+z^2).  
 (b) Show that h extends meromorphically through every \rho \in S and that the extension coincides with the constant 1; in particular h\equiv 1 on \mathbb{C}.

Determine all functions that satisfy the system (*) and provide complete, rigorous proofs of every assertion.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we use the matrices  

 M(z)=1  z  0   b(z)=1+z,   X(z)=f(z)  ,  
    0  1  1-z     2-z       f(1-z),  
    -z 0  1     1-z       f(-z)  ,

so that (*) is equivalent to M(z)X(z)=b(z).

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Equivalence (*) \Leftrightarrow  \Delta (z)[f(z)-1]=0    

Step 1. det M(z).  
A cofactor expansion gives  

  det M(z)=1-z^2+z^3=:\Delta (z).  (1)

Step 2. Cramer's rule for f(z).  
Replace the first column of M(z) by b(z) and call the new matrix M_1(z).  
Again by a cofactor expansion  

  det M_1(z)=1-z^2+z^3=\Delta (z).  

Hence  

  \Delta (z)\cdot f(z)=det M_1(z)=\Delta (z) \Rightarrow  \Delta (z)[f(z)-1]=0  for all z\in \mathbb{C}. (2)

Step 3. Converse.  
If an entire f satisfies (2) then f(z)=1 on \mathbb{C}\S, an open set possessing an
accumulation point; by the Identity Theorem f\equiv 1.  Substituting f\equiv 1
into (*) confirms the three relations, whence (*) and (2) are equivalent.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Uniqueness of the entire solution  

Part 1 already enforces f\equiv 1; consequently the full coupled system (I)-(III)
admits exactly one entire (indeed meromorphic) solution:

    f(z)=1  (\forall z\in \mathbb{C}).

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Selective rigidity

Put h(z):=g(z)-1.  The three relations become homogeneous equations for h.

3(a) Equation (I).  
(I) gives h(z)+z h(1-z)=0.  Replacing z by 1-z and eliminating h(1-z)
yields  

  (1-z+z^2) h(z)=0=:D(z) h(z).  

Because h is entire, D(z) h(z)\equiv 0 implies h vanishes identically: outside
its two isolated zeros \zeta _+,\zeta _- of D the factor in front of h is non-zero,
and the Identity Theorem forces h\equiv 0.  Hence g\equiv 1.

3(b) Equation (III).  
(III) becomes  

  h(-z)-z h(z)=0.  (3)

Replacing z by -z and substituting the first relation into the second one
gives  

  (1+z^2) h(z)=0.  

Again h is entire, so it must vanish identically; whence g\equiv 1.

3(c) Equation (II) - an infinite-dimensional solution space.  

The inhomogeneous equation  

  h(1-z)+(1-z) h(-z)=0   (4)

does not force h to vanish.  We construct an explicit family of
non-trivial entire solutions.  Let \Phi  be any entire function satisfying  

  \Phi (1-z)=\Phi (-z)  for every z\in \mathbb{C}.  (5)

(An abundance of such functions exists; for instance every exponential
\Phi _n(z)=e^{2\pi  i n z} (n\in \mathbb{Z}) has this property because
e^{2\pi  i n(1-z)}=e^{2\pi  i n}e^{-2\pi  i n z}=e^{-2\pi  i n z}=\Phi _n(-z).)

Define  

  h_\Phi (z):=\Gamma (z+1)\sin(\pi  z) \Phi (z),    (6)

and put  

  g_\Phi (z):=1+h_\Phi (z).        (7)

Entirety of h_\Phi .
\Gamma (z+1) has simple poles at the negative integers, while sin \pi z has the
same zeros; hence their product is an entire function.  Multiplying by
the entire \Phi  preserves entire-ness.

Verification of (4).  
We use the two standard formulas  

  \Gamma (2-z)=(1-z) \Gamma (1-z),    sin(\pi (1-z))=sin \pi z.  (8)

From (6) and (8) we obtain  

h_\Phi (1-z)=(1-z) \Gamma (1-z) sin(\pi  z) \Phi (1-z),  
h_\Phi (-z)=-\Gamma (1-z) sin(\pi  z) \Phi (-z).

Because \Phi  satisfies (5), \Phi (1-z)=\Phi (-z), whence  

h_\Phi (1-z)+(1-z) h_\Phi (-z)  
  = (1-z) \Gamma (1-z) sin(\pi  z) \Phi (-z)  
    -(1-z) \Gamma (1-z) sin(\pi  z) \Phi (-z)  = 0.

Thus every g_\Phi  defined in (7) satisfies equation (II).  Varying the
entire function \Phi  subject to (5) produces an infinite-dimensional family
of solutions; for \Phi \equiv 1 we recover the elementary example

  g(z)=1+\Gamma (z+1)\sin(\pi  z)

mentioned in the review.

Therefore equation (II) alone is flexible: it has infinitely many
entire solutions, none of which (except the constant one) satisfies
either (I) or (III).

3(d) Consequences.  
* Relations (I) and (III) are rigid: every entire solution equals 1;  
 this solution necessarily fulfils the other two relations, so the whole
 system (*) holds.  
* Relation (II) is flexible: it possesses an infinite-dimensional space
 of entire solutions and does not imply (I) or (III).

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Meromorphic solutions defined on U  

Put k(z)=h(z)-1.  Throughout we only use relation (I); hence again the
polynomial  

  D(z)=1-z+z^2  (9)

reappears.

Equation (I) reads on U  

  k(z)+z k(1-z)=0, (10)  

and replacing z by 1-z gives  

  k(1-z)+(1-z) k(z)=0. (11)

Eliminating k(1-z) from (10)-(11) gives  

  D(z) k(z)=0.   (12)

4(a) The zeros \zeta _+, \zeta _- of D.  
Outside the points \zeta \pm  the factor D(z) is non-zero, so (12) forces
k\equiv 0.  Near \zeta \pm  the same argument as in the original solution shows that h
is holomorphic and assumes the value 1 at each of \zeta \pm .

4(b) Extension across the roots of \Delta .  
Let \rho \in S.  Because \rho \notin U, h is a priori undefined at \rho , but U contains a
punctured neighbourhood of \rho .  On U we already know k\equiv 0, so
lim_{z\to \rho ,z\in U}k(z)=0.  If h had a pole at \rho  the same limit would be
infinite - contradiction.  Hence \rho  is a removable singularity of h with
value 1.  Doing this for every root of \Delta  yields an entire extension that
equals 1 on U and therefore everywhere.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Answer.  The coupled system (I)-(III) possesses exactly one entire---and in
fact one meromorphic---solution:

      f(z)=1  for every z\in \mathbb{C}. \blacksquare

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
•  Multiple equations  –   The original problem has a single
functional equation; here one must satisfy {\em three} coupled relations
simultaneously.

•  Three interacting arguments  –   
The unknown values \(f(z),\,f(1-z),\,f(-z)\) appear together, forcing a
\(3\times3\) linear system instead of the \(2\times2\) system in the
kernel variant.  This raises both algebraic complexity and bookkeeping.

•  Non-trivial determinant  –  
Solving demands computation of a cubic determinant
\(\Delta(z)=1-z^{2}+z^{3}\) whose vanishing introduces a {\em singular
locus} of three complex points.  One must treat the regular and
singular cases separately, recognise the rank drop, and manage the
resulting free parameters.

•  Rational–function core  –   
Outside the singular set the solution is a genuinely {\em rational}
function of degree three over degree three, rather than the constant
solution of the original problem.  Obtaining it requires systematic
elimination, not mere substitution.

•  Parametrisation on the singular set  –   
At each root of \(\Delta\) the system loses rank, so compatibility must be
checked and the local freedom carefully parametrised; this demands a
full analysis of linear dependence, something entirely absent in the
original exercise.

In summary, the problem keeps the original idea
“relate \(f\) at two (now three) symmetric points and isolate a
multiplicative polynomial”, but it escalates the dimension, the number
of constraints, the algebraic labour, and the subtlety of the singular
case, making it significantly harder than both the original and the
current kernel variant.
